g.data-narration: Welcome to Register Allocation in Microcontrollers. This topic covers efficient use of limited CPU registers in 8051 microcontrollers.
Register allocation is the process of efficiently assigning variables to CPU registers to minimize slow memory access operations.
g.text: The process of efficiently assigning variables and data to CPU registers to minimize
memory access operations. In 8051: Register operations take 1-2 cycles, while
internal RAM takes 2-4 cycles, external RAM takes 4-8 cycles.

g.data-narration: Microcontrollers have severely limited registers - 8051 has only 8 GPRs per bank with 4 banks total. Efficient usage is critical.
g.text: • Limited Resources: 8051 has only 8 GPRs (R0-R7) per bank
• 4 Register Banks available: 32 registers total, but only ONE active at a time
• Every register decision impacts performance and code efficiency

g.data-narration: The 8051 has four register banks at memory locations 00H-1FH. Each bank contains R0-R7, selected via PSW register bits.
PSW register bits RS0 (PSW.3) and RS1 (PSW.4) control bank selection. Formula: Bank = (RS1 × 2) + RS0.
GPRs include R0-R7 in active bank, plus accumulator (A) and B register. A is most used - serves as implicit operand in many instructions.
g.text: operand in ADD, SUBB, MOV, etc.

g.data-narration: SFRs control peripheral functions. Key ones: PSW (flags, bank select), SP (stack pointer), DPTR (16-bit for external memory).
g.text: SP defaults to 07H - overlaps Bank 0! Initialize SP to 60H+
DPTR is the ONLY 16-bit register - essential for external memory access

g.data-narration: Two conventions: Caller-saved (caller preserves registers before call), Callee-saved (subroutine preserves registers it modifies).
g.text: making subroutine call. Caller manages preservation.
SUB: PUSH ACC ; Save at start

g.data-narration: Parameters can be passed through registers (faster, limited) or stack (slower, flexible). 8051 typically uses A and B for parameters.
g.text: Faster but limited by available registers.
Use R0-R7, ACC, B for parameters.
More flexible but slower. Push parameters
Trade-off: Register passing = faster code, fewer registers available | Stack passing = flexible, slower execution

g.data-narration: Optimize by: reusing registers when values are no longer needed, using bank switching instead of stack saving, organizing code to minimize conflicts.
g.text: Free registers when values no longer needed
Switch banks instead of pushing to stack
Group related operations to use same registers

g.data-narration: Example: Switch to Bank 1 before subroutine call to preserve Bank 0 registers without pushing to stack. Uses less stack space.
g.text: ; Save current bank, switch to Bank 1
• Faster than pushing 8 registers to stack

g.data-narration: Example: Pass parameters in R0 and R1, perform addition, return result in accumulator. Faster than stack-based passing.
g.text: ADD A, R1 ; Result in A
No need for explicit return statement.

g.data-narration: Example: Subroutine saves R2, R3 at start, restores before return. Caller's values preserved without caller knowing.
Key formulas: Bank = (RS1×2)+RS0. SP initialization: 60H+. Register cycles: Reg(1-2), RAM(2-4), Ext(4-8).
Summary: Register allocation is critical for efficient 8051 code. Use banks, save registers properly, prefer callee-saved convention, initialize SP.
g.text: • 8051 has 4 banks (R0-R7 each), select via PSW.3(RS0) and PSW.4(RS1)
• Use callee-saved convention for cleaner code | Initialize SP to 60H+
• Pass parameters via registers when possible | Save ACC, B, PSW in ISRs
